blocks:
Ayahs

وَيْلٌ لِّكُلِّ هُمَزَةٍ لُّمَزَةٍ (١)
Woe to every scandal-monger and slanderer, (1)
ٱلَّذِى جَمَعَ مَالًا وَعَدَّدَهُۥ (٢)
who amasses wealth and counts it over. (2)
يَحْسَبُ أَنَّ مَالَهُۥٓ أَخْلَدَهُۥ (٣)
He supposes his wealth will make him immortal! (3)
كَلَّا ۖ لَيُنۢبَذَنَّ فِى ٱلْحُطَمَةِ (٤)
No indeed! He will surely be cast into the Crusher. (4)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلْحُطَمَةُ (٥)
And what will show you what is the Crusher? (5)
نَارُ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُوقَدَةُ (٦)
[It is] the fire of Allah, set ablaze, (6)
ٱلَّتِى تَطَّلِعُ عَلَى ٱلْأَفْـِٔدَةِ (٧)
which will overspread the hearts. (7)
إِنَّهَا عَلَيْهِم مُّؤْصَدَةٌ (٨)
Indeed it will close in upon them (8)
فِى عَمَدٍ مُّمَدَّدَةٍۭ (٩)
in outstretched columns. (9)

Content and Virtue of Surah al-Humazah

This Surah, which is among the Meccan Surahs, speaks of those who direct all their efforts toward the accumulation of wealth and summarize all human existential values within it. Consequently, they look upon those whose hands are empty of wealth with contempt, subjecting them to ridicule.

These arrogant wealth-accumulators and deceitful egoists become so intoxicated by the wine of pride and vanity that they find pleasure and recreation in belittling others, fault-finding, mockery, and backbiting.

At the end of the Surah, it speaks of their painful destiny—how they will be cast into Hell in a humiliating manner, and how the blazing fire of Jahannam will first seize their hearts, setting fire to their souls and spirits, which were the centers of all that pride, vanity, and wickedness; a fire that is burning, continuous, and prolonged.

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله states:

من قرأ سورة الهمزة اعطى من الاجر عشر حسنات بعدد من استهزأ بمحمد (ص) و اصحابه:

"Whoever recites this Surah will be given ten good deeds as a reward for every person who mocked Muhammad and his companions." [^1] And in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever recites it in an obligatory prayer, poverty will be distanced from him, sustenance will turn toward him, and ugly and bad deaths will be repelled from him." ^2

[^1] and ^2: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 536.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ وَيْلٌ لِكُلِّ هُمَزَةٍ لُمَزَةٍ (1) الَّذِي جَمَعَ مالاً وَ عَدَّدَهُ (2) يَحْسَبُ أَنَّ مالَهُ أَخْلَدَهُ (3) كَلاَّ لَيُنْبَذَنَّ فِي الْحُطَمَةِ (4) وَ ما أَدْراكَ مَا الْحُطَمَةُ (5) نارُ اللَّهِ الْمُوقَدَةُ (6) الَّتِي تَطَّلِعُ عَلَى الْأَفْئِدَةِ (7) إِنَّها عَلَيْهِمْ مُؤْصَدَةٌ (8) فِي عَمَدٍ مُمَدَّدَةٍ (9)

Occasion of Revelation

A group of commentators have stated that the verses of this Surah were revealed concerning "Walid ibn al-Mughirah," who used to backbite the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله behind his back and taunt and mock him to his face.

Others have considered it to be about other leaders of polytheism and prominent, spiteful enemies of Islam, such as "Akhnas ibn Shariq," "Umayyah ibn Khalaf," and "As ibn Wa'il."

However, even if we accept these occasions of revelation, the generality of the verses' meaning is not broken; rather, it includes all those who possess these characteristics.

Commentary

Woe to the Fault-finders and Backbiters!

This Surah begins with a crushing threat, saying: "Woe to every mocking fault-finder!" (وَيْلٌ لِكُلِّ هُمَزَةٍ لُمَزَةٍ). Those who, with the sting of their tongue and movements of their hands, eyes, and eyebrows, mock others behind their backs or to their faces, or engage in fault-finding and backbiting, or make them targets for the arrows of taunt and slander.

"Humazah" and "Lumazah" are both intensive forms (sighah al-mubalaghah). [^1] The first is from the root "hamz," which originally means "to break," and since fault-finders and backbiters break the character of others, they are called "Humazah."

And "Lumazah" is from the root "lamz" (on the pattern of ramz), which originally means to backbite and find fault.

Regarding whether these two words have the same meaning—referring to backbiters and fault-finders—or if there is a difference between them, commentators have offered many possibilities. Some have taken them to have the same meaning, thus mentioning them together is for emphasis.

However, some have said "Humazah" means backbiter and "Lumazah" means fault-finder.

Others have considered "Humazah" to refer to those who find fault with gestures of the hand and head, and "Lumazah" to those who do so with the tongue.

Some consider the "first" to refer to fault-finding to one's face, and the "second" to fault-finding behind one's back.

Some have counted the first as open fault-finding and the second as hidden fault-finding with the wink of an eye or movement of an eyebrow.

And it has sometimes been said that both mean one who refers to people with ugly and offensive nicknames.

Finally, in a statement from Ibn Abbas, it is narrated that he said in the interpretation of these two: "They are those who walk with slander (namimah), who separate loved ones, and who describe people with faults." [^1] It seems Ibn Abbas derived this from a Hadith narrated from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, where He said:

"Shall I inform you of the worst among you?" They said: "Yes, O Messenger of Allah!" He said: "Those who walk much with slander, who separate loved ones, and who seek faults for the innocent." ^2 However, from the collective words of lexicographers, it is understood that these two words are of the same meaning and have a broad concept that includes every kind of fault-finding, backbiting, taunting, and mockery by means of the tongue, signs, gestures, slander, and speaking ill.

[^1]: The intensive form (sighah al-mubalaghah) appears in patterns other than the six well-known ones, including this pattern, which has parallels in the Arabic language such as "duhakah," meaning one who laughs a lot.

[^1]: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 32, p. 92. ^2: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on Slander (al-Namimah), Hadith 1.

In any case, the expression "Woe" (Wayl) is a severe threat toward this group. Fundamentally, the verses of the Quran take a harsh stance against such individuals, using expressions not seen for any other sin. For example, after threatening the blind-hearted hypocrites with a painful punishment for mocking the believers, He says:

"Whether you ask forgiveness for them or do not ask forgiveness for them—if you ask forgiveness for them seventy times—Allah will never forgive them" (At-Tawbah: 80).

A similar meaning regarding the hypocrites who mocked the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله appears in Surah al-Munafiqun, verse 5.

Fundamentally, the honor and dignity of individuals are highly respected in Islam, and any action that leads to the belittling of people is a great sin. In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated: "The most abject of people is he who insults people!" [^1] We have provided more detail in this regard under verses 11 and 12 of Surah al-Hujurat (Vol. 22, pp. 177 to 195).

Then, He addresses the source of this ugly act (fault-finding and mockery), which often stems from the pride and vanity born of wealth, adding: "The one who collected wealth and counted it," without considering whether it was legitimate or illegitimate (الَّذِي جَمَعَ مالًا وَ عَدَّدَهُ). He loves wealth and riches so much that he constantly counts them, finding pleasure and joy in the glitter of dirhams, dinars, and other money.

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 75, p. 142.

Every dirham and dinar is an idol to him; he sees not only his own personality but all personalities summarized within it. It is natural that such a lost and foolish human would constantly subject poor believers to mockery.

"Addadah" is originally from the root "add," meaning to count. Some have also suggested it is from "uddah" (on the pattern of ghuddah), meaning to prepare these properties and store them for difficulties and a rainy day.

Some have also interpreted it as withholding and keeping.

However, the first meaning is the most apparent of all.

In any case, the verse refers to wealth-accumulators who view wealth not as a means but as an end, and who observe no limits or conditions in its collection. They gather it from the lawful and the prohibited, from encroaching on the rights of others, through honorable means or through base and vile ways, and they consider it the only sign of greatness and personality.

They do not want wealth to meet the needs of life, and for this reason, the more their wealth increases, the more their greed grows. Otherwise, wealth within reasonable limits and from legitimate sources is not only not condemned, but in the Holy Quran, it is sometimes referred to as "the Bounty of Allah" (Fadl Allah), where He says: "And seek of the bounty of Allah" (Al-Jumu'ah: 10). Elsewhere, He refers to it as "Good" (Khayr): "It is prescribed for you, when death approaches one of you, if he leaves behind good (wealth), to make a bequest."

Such wealth is certainly neither a cause for rebellion, nor a means of boasting, nor an excuse for mocking others. But the wealth that is a deity and the ultimate goal, and which invites its owners to rebellion like "Qarun," is a disgrace, a humiliation, a calamity, and a misfortune, leading to distance from God and eternity in the fire of Hell.

And often, the collection of a large quantity of this wealth is not possible except through much contamination. Therefore, in a Hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام, we read that he said:

"Wealth does not accumulate except with five characteristics: extreme stinginess, long-reaching hopes, overwhelming greed, severing of family ties, and preferring this world over the hereafter." [^1] This is because those who are generous and are not trapped by long-reaching hopes are careful about the lawful and prohibited, help their relatives, and usually, wealth does not accumulate with them, even if their income is high.

In the next verse, He adds: "This gold-hoarding and wealth-worshipping human thinks his wealth will make him immortal" (يَحْسَبُ أَنَّ مالَهُ أَخْلَدَهُ). ^2 It is interesting that "akhlada" (made immortal) appears here in the "past tense" form, meaning he thinks his wealth has already turned him into an immortal being; neither can death come for him, nor can illnesses and the events of the world create a problem for him, because in his view, the only solver of problems is wealth, and he holds this problem-solver in his hand.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 668, Hadith 7.

What a false notion and raw fantasy! The wealth that was so much at the disposal of Qarun that several strong men struggled to carry the keys to his treasures—yet when the divine punishment struck, they could not delay his death by a single hour, and Allah swallowed him and his treasures into the earth in an instant with a minor earthquake: "So We caused the earth to swallow him and his home" (Al-Qasas: 81).

Wealth, the perfect example of which was in the hands of the Pharaohs of Egypt, yet as the verse says: "How many gardens and springs they left behind, and cornfields and noble sites, and pleasant things wherein they took delight" (Ad-Dukhan: 25-27). But all of this easily passed to others within an hour: "Thus [it was], and We caused it to be inherited by another people" (Ad-Dukhan: 28).

Therefore, on the Day of Resurrection, when the veils are removed, they will realize their great mistake and their cry will rise: "My wealth has not availed me; my authority has gone from me" (Al-Haqqah: 28-29).

Fundamentally, man detests annihilation and non-existence and is a proponent of eternity and immortality. This internal desire helps us in the discussions of the Hereafter to know that man was created for immortality; otherwise, the instinct of the love for immortality would not be in him.

However, this arrogant, selfish, and world-worshipping human sometimes sees his immortality in things that are exactly the cause of his annihilation; for instance, he considers wealth and status, which are often the enemies of his survival, as the means of immortality.

From this explanation, it becomes clear that the notion of immortality through wealth is considered a reason for accumulating wealth, and accumulating wealth is a factor for mocking and ridiculing others in the eyes of these blind-hearted people.

The Quran, in response to this group, says: "It is not as he thinks" (كَلَّا). "Rather, he will surely be thrown with complete humiliation and abjectness into a crushing fire!" (لَيُنْبَذَنَّ فِي الْحُطَمَةِ).

Then He interprets "Hutamah" as follows: "And what will make you know what the Hutamah is?" (وَ ما أَدْراكَ مَا الْحُطَمَةُ).

"It is the kindled Fire of Allah" (نارُ اللَّهِ الْمُوقَدَةُ).

"The fire which leaps over the hearts," and its first sparks appear in the hearts! (الَّتِي تَطَّلِعُ عَلَى الْأَفْئِدَةِ). "Layunbadhanna" is from the root "nabdh," which according to Raghib in "Al-Mufradat" originally means to throw something away because of its insignificance and worthlessness.

That is, Allah will throw these arrogant, selfish, self-superior individuals on that Day into the fire of Hell as abject and worthless beings, so they may see the result of their pride and vanity.

"Hutamah" is an intensive form from the root "hatm," meaning to break something into pieces. This shows that the blazing fire of Hell severely breaks their limbs. However, it is understood from some narrations that "Hutamah" is not the name of the entire Hell, but the name of an exceptionally burning section of it. [^1] Understanding this meaning—that fire breaks limbs instead of just burning them—might have been difficult in the past, but today, as the issue of the intensity of the impact of blast waves is clear to us all—how the waves from a massive explosion can break not only humans but also strong iron bars and massive building columns—it is not a strange matter.

The expression "Nar Allah" (the Fire of Allah) is evidence of its greatness, and the expression "muqadah" is evidence of its eternal blazing.

Strangely, this fire, unlike all the fires of this world which first burn the skin and then penetrate inside, first strikes the hearts and burns the interior; first the heart, then the brain and bone, and then it spreads to the outside.

What kind of fire is this whose first sparks appear in the heart of man? What kind of fire is this that burns the inside before the outside? Everything about the Resurrection is strange and differs greatly from this world, even the engagement of its blazing fire!

Why should it not be so? While their hearts were the center of disbelief, pride, and vanity, and the center of the love for the world, wealth, and property.

Why should the fire of Divine wrath and anger not seize their hearts before anything else, while they burned the hearts of the believers in this world with mockery, fault-finding, backbiting, and humiliation? Divine justice requires that they see a punishment similar to their deeds.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 3, pp. 17 and 19, Hadith 60 and 64.

In the final verses of this Surah, He says: "Indeed, it is closed in upon them!" (إِنَّها عَلَيْهِمْ مُؤْصَدَةٌ).

"Mu'sadah" is from the root "isad," meaning to close a door and secure it. Therefore, the small chambers created inside mountains for collecting wealth were called "wasid."

In truth, just as they kept their wealth in safes and closed repositories, Allah also imprisons them in the closed punishment of Hell, from which there is no way of escape or salvation!

And finally, He says: "They will be in extended and long columns" (فِي عَمَدٍ مُمَدَّدَةٍ). "Amad" is the plural of "amud," meaning a column or any long object like pieces of wood or iron, and "mumaddadah" means extended and long.

A group of commentators have considered this expression to refer to massive iron nails with which the doors of Hell are firmly closed, such that there is absolutely no way out of it; thus, it is an emphasis on the previous verse which says the doors of Hell are closed upon them and they are hemmed in from every side.

Some have also considered it a reference to a type of instrument of punishment and penalty, similar to what is known among us as "stocks and chains," which is a heavy piece of wood or iron with two indentations the size of ankles; the feet were placed in it, and it was covered with a bar and locked, so that the person had no power to move. This is the recompense for their torture of innocent people in this world.

Some have also mentioned a third interpretation with the help of recent discoveries, which is that the blazing flames of Hell will dominate them in the form of extended and long columns. They say it has been proven in recent discoveries that special X-rays (Roentgen), unlike other rays that spread in a conical shape, are emitted in a cylindrical shape exactly like a column. Strangely, these rays penetrate the entire existence of man and even dominate the heart, and for this reason, they are used for photographing internal organs. It appears that a ray rises from the blazing fire of Hell that is not unlike the aforementioned ray. [^1] However, among these interpretations, the most suitable is the first interpretation (of course, according to some of these interpretations, the phrase "فِي عَمَدٍ مُمَدَّدَةٍ" describes the state of Hell, and in others, it describes the state of the people of Hell).

Key Points

1- Pride and Vanity: The Source of Great Sins

"Self-superiority" is a great affliction that is considered the raw material for many sins. Negligence of God, ingratitude for blessings, drowning in luxury and hedonism, belittling others, and mocking believers are all ominous effects of this vile trait. People of low capacity, as soon as they attain some means, become so trapped by pride and vanity that they grant absolutely no value to others, and that becomes the cause of their separation from society and society's separation from them.

They sink into a world of fantasy, imagining themselves as a "separate weave" and even counting themselves among those close to God. This causes the honor, dignity, and even the lives of others to be worthless and insignificant in their eyes. They engage in "hamz" and "lamz," and by fault-finding and blaming others, they imagine they are increasing their own greatness! Interestingly, in some narrations, such individuals are likened to "scorpions" whose job is to sting (and if the scorpion's sting is not out of malice, their sting is out of spite).

In a Hadith, it is mentioned that the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: "On the night of Mi'raj, I saw a group of the people of Hell whose flesh was being torn from their sides and fed to them! I asked Gabriel: 'Who are these?' He said: 'These are the fault-finders and mockers from your Ummah!'" [^1] As we pointed out above, we had a detailed discussion in this regard under the verses of Surah al-Hujurat.

2- Greed for Accumulating Wealth

Regarding wealth and riches, there are different opinions ranging toward extremes of excess and neglect. Some grant it such importance that they consider it the solver of all their problems, to the point that proponents of this view have spoken at length in their poetry. Among them, an Arabic poet says:

The eloquence of Sahban, the calligraphy of Ibn Muqlah, The wisdom of Luqman, and the asceticism of Ibn Adham— If they all gather in a man, yet the man is penniless, He has no value, not even the value of a dirham!

[^1]: Tantawi, under the verses being discussed. [^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 667, Hadith 5.

Therefore, it is no surprise that this group constantly engages in accumulating property, never resting for a moment, observing no limits or conditions, and seeing the lawful and prohibited as equal.

At the opposite point of this group is a community that grants wealth and riches the least value; they praise poverty and value it, even considering wealth an obstacle to piety and closeness to God! However, between these two opinions which lie at the extremes of "excess" and "neglect," what is understood from the Holy Quran and Islamic narrations is that wealth is good, but on several conditions: first, that it be a "means" and not an "end."

Second, that it does not make man its "prisoner," but rather man should be the "commander" over it.

Third, that it be obtained through legitimate means and spent in the way of God's pleasure.

Love for such wealth is not only not world-worshipping, but it is evidence of love for the Hereafter. Therefore, in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: when the Imam cursed gold and silver (dhahab and fiddah), one of the companions was surprised and asked about this. The Imam عليه السلام said:

"It is not as you think. Rather, 'dhahab' (gold) is that which takes away (dhahaba bi) the religion, and 'fiddah' (silver) is that which overflows (afadat) with disbelief." [^1]

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 73, p. 141, Hadith 17.

In another Hadith from Imam Amir al-Mu'minin عليه السلام, we read:

"Intoxication is of four kinds: the intoxication of wine, the intoxication of wealth, the intoxication of sleep, and the intoxication of power!" [^1] In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is narrated that a man came to him and said: "May my father and mother be sacrificed for you, give me an exhortation." He said:

"If good deeds are true, then why the accumulation? And if the compensation from Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, is true, then why the stinginess?!" ^2 Many are those who are busy accumulating wealth until the end of their lives, and finally leave it for others; they must give the account for it, while the benefit belongs to others. Therefore, we read in a Hadith that Amir al-Mu'minin Ali عليه السلام was asked: "Who among people has the greatest regret?" He said: "The one who sees his wealth in the scale of another, and Allah enters him into the Fire because of it, while He enters his heir into Paradise because of it!" [^3] And in another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of the verse "Thus Allah will show them their deeds as regrets for them," he said:

"This is about a man who leaves wealth and does not spend it in obedience to Allah out of stinginess, then he dies and leaves it for someone who acts with it in obedience to Allah or in disobedience to Him."

Then the Imam عليه السلام added: "If he spends it in the way of obedience to God, he (the original owner) sees it in the scale of another's deeds and feels regret, because the wealth was his wealth; and if he spends it in disobedience to God, it has become a means of strengthening him to sin (and again the punishment and regret return to him)." [^1] Yes, the stance of humans toward wealth varies; sometimes they make a dangerous idol out of it, and sometimes a means of great happiness.

We conclude this discussion with a meaningful Hadith from Ibn Abbas; he says:

"When the first dirham and dinar coins were struck on earth, Iblis looked at them. When he viewed them, he took them and placed them on his eyes, then pressed them to his chest, then let out a cry, then pressed them to his chest again, then said: 'You two are the delight of my eyes and the fruit of my heart! I do not care if the children of Adam do not worship an idol as long as they love you two! It is enough for me from the children of Adam that they love you.'" (Because you are the greatest idol). ^2

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 73, p. 142. ^2: Tawhid al-Saduq, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 668, Hadith 8. [^3]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 73, p. 142. [^1]: Same source, Hadith 20. ^2: Same source, p. 137, Hadith 3.

O Allah! Protect us from the intoxication of wealth, status, the world, and desires. O Lord! Deliver us from the dominion of Satan and the servitude of dirhams and dinars.

O God! The fire of Hell is severely crushing, and escape from it is not possible except by Your grace; include us in Your grace.

Amin, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah al-Humazah.
